metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the introspection HTTP call uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (async, `await client.post(...)`) — not `httpx.Client` — so the verify coroutine is truly non-blocking in the async FastMCP event loop.
evidence: 'Reuse the already-present httpx' — async vs. sync client variant is unspecified
src/rebar/llm/runner.py uses httpx.AsyncClient for async HTTP in an async context
MCP server is async (FastMCP / ASGI); blocking the event loop stalls all concurrent connections for the full connect+read timeout window
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'reuse the already-present httpx' for the introspection POST but never states whether to use httpx.AsyncClient (async, non-blocking) or httpx.Client (sync, blocks the event loop). The MCP server is an async ASGI app (FastMCP); calling a synchronous httpx.Client in the async verify path blocks the event loop for every concurrent MCP connection for up to the full read timeout (10 s). The existing codebase consistently uses httpx.AsyncClient for async contexts (runner.py). If the implementer defaults to the simpler synchronous client, every introspection call becomes an event-loop-stalling operation — a correctness defect, not a performance concern.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section / Security section ('bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)')
scenarios: Implementer uses httpx.Client (the simpler API) in the async verify coroutine → event loop blocked for up to 15 s per introspection call under any load.
Two concurrent MCP tool calls both trigger token verification → both stall until the first introspection completes, turning a 10 ms tool into a 10 s operation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The introspection POST uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (`await client.post(...)`) to remain non-blocking in the FastMCP async event loop; a synchronous `httpx.Client` would block the loop for the full timeout duration under concurrent load.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether httpx is used in async or sync mode for the introspection HTTP call. If sync httpx is used inside an async MCP server (the expected runtime), every token validation blocks the event loop for up to 15 s (connect 5 s + read 10 s timeout), degrading all concurrent requests during that window.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security (timeout spec) / 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (not the sync client) so the connect-5s/read-10s timeout is enforced via `asyncio` without blocking the event loop. The client is instantiated once at construction and closed via an async `aclose()` on server shutdown.'

findings.finding: The plan says 'reuse the already-present httpx' but never specifies whether the introspection HTTP call uses httpx.AsyncClient (non-blocking, correct for the async MCP server runtime) or httpx.Client (blocking, would stall the event loop on every token validation). The MCP server runs under an asyncio event loop (FastMCP), and the existing codebase exclusively uses httpx.AsyncClient for HTTP within async contexts. An implementer following only this plan could choose sync httpx and produce a verifier that serializes all request handling through a single blocking OS thread per introspection call — a silent correctness/performance defect that appears functional under low load.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'The HTTP call uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (async POST, `await`) so the verifier is a true `async def` coroutine and never blocks the MCP server's event loop.'

findings.finding: The plan says 'reuse the already-present httpx' for the introspection POST but never specifies whether the call uses httpx.AsyncClient (non-blocking, correct for an async MCP server) or httpx.Client (sync, blocks the event loop per request). The MCP server is async (FastMCP); a synchronous httpx.Client.post() inside an async verify() body blocks the event loop for every token introspection under load.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Security ('bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, replace 'a bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)' with 'a bounded HTTP timeout via `httpx.AsyncClient` (async, non-blocking; connect 5s / read 10s)'. In AC4, add an observable oracle: 'the verify() method is a coroutine (async def) and the stateful fake is a mock async handler'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the httpx client instantiated for introspection calls must suppress body-level logging (e.g., via a custom event hook that redacts the `token` parameter, or by instantiating the client without default log-hooks), so the 'tokens/secrets never logged' guarantee holds even at DEBUG log levels.
evidence: 'Tokens/secrets never logged' — declared as a security requirement
The introspection POST body contains `token=<bearer-token-value>` per RFC 7662 §2.1
httpx logs request bodies at DEBUG level by default unless event hooks or a sanitising transport are used
The plan does not mention disabling or sanitising httpx request/response logging on the introspection client
findings.finding: The outbound introspection POST carries the raw bearer token in the request body (`token=<value>`), but the plan does not specify that the httpx client used must suppress body-level logging. At debug log levels (common in development/staging), httpx can log request bodies, exposing the bearer token — violating the plan's own 'tokens/secrets never logged' contract on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Security section — 'Tokens/secrets never logged'; Approach & rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
scenarios: An operator runs the MCP server with DEBUG logging enabled (common during onboarding or incident investigation); httpx logs the full POST body including the bearer token, leaking user credentials to logs.
A logging aggregator captures httpx debug output; bearer tokens become queryable in the log store, violating least-exposure and enabling token replay.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The httpx client used for introspection MUST NOT log request or response bodies; instantiate it with a sanitising event hook or a dedicated transport that strips the `token` field from any log output, regardless of the ambient log level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the 'malformed response' definition in the Security section and the corresponding AC to cover semantically invalid `exp` (non-positive integer, non-integer type) and unexpected `aud` type (not a string or array) — specify that these also raise (fail-closed) rather than producing `expires_at=0` or silently accepting/rejecting.
evidence: 'a malformed response — a 200 whose body is not valid JSON, or valid JSON lacking a boolean active field — raises' — malformed definition does not include semantically invalid exp or unexpected aud type
'Never emit expires_at=0 (the SDK reads a falsy value as no-expiry while looking like a set value)' — an AS returning exp=0 or exp=-1 is not covered by the malformed guard
RFC 7662 §2.2 defines exp as a JSON number (integer seconds since epoch) but the introspection response is AS-controlled and may supply unexpected types
findings.finding: The plan's 'malformed response' category (which triggers a fail-closed raise) covers only non-JSON bodies and missing/non-boolean `active`, but does NOT cover semantically invalid `exp` values — negative integers, zero, or non-integer types (e.g., a string `"never"`). The plan separately prohibits emitting `expires_at=0`, yet a malformed `exp` (e.g., `exp: 0`, `exp: -1`, or `exp: "tomorrow"`) could either produce a prohibited `expires_at=0` or an uncontrolled `ValueError`/`TypeError` rather than a controlled raise, depending on implementation. Similarly, a non-string/non-array `aud` (e.g., a number) is not covered by the accept/reject logic, leaving an uncontrolled exception path rather than a clean fail-closed raise.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport: a non-200 status, a timeout, or a malformed response … raises'; Security section — 'Never emit expires_at=0'; AC line — 'Unit tests assert rejection for … mismatched aud'
scenarios: An AS returns `{"active": true, "exp": 0}` (epoch time, effectively already-expired); the plan prohibits `expires_at=0` but this value passes the malformed guard and may be silently stored, giving the SDK a falsy value it misreads as no-expiry.

An AS returns `{"active": true, "exp": "2099-01-01"}` (ISO string instead of Unix epoch); an unguarded `int()` cast raises `ValueError` which may or may not be caught depending on implementation, potentially causing a 500 instead of a clean deny.
An AS returns `{"active": true, "aud": 42}` (integer instead of string or array); the string/array branch logic skips it, leaving ambiguous behaviour.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section under 'Fail-closed transport': 'A semantically invalid `exp` — zero, negative, or a non-integer type — is treated as malformed and raises (never stored as `expires_at=0` or `expires_at` of a negative value). An `aud` field that is neither a JSON string nor a JSON array is treated as a malformed response and raises.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the startup behavior when `auth_introspection_endpoint` is absent or empty while `introspection` is in `mcp.auth_strategies`, and add it to AC#5's oracle (expected: fail-closed ConfigError at construction, matching the secret-absent pattern).
evidence: Security section: 'the client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env, which MUST be present and non-empty at startup or the verifier refuses to start — fail-closed'
No parallel statement for auth_introspection_endpoint being absent/empty
AC#5: only covers secret-env-var absent/empty; no oracle for missing endpoint
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens at startup when `introspection` is present in `mcp.auth_strategies` but `auth_introspection_endpoint` is not configured. The secret-absent case is explicitly fail-closed (verifier refuses to start), but the analogous endpoint-absent case has no stated behavior — the fallback for this incomplete sub-step is unspecified.
findings.location: Security section (construction-time guards) and AC#5
scenarios: Operator sets auth_strategies=['introspection'] but forgets auth_introspection_endpoint — unclear if startup raises a clear error or silently skips the verifier or attempts to POST to an empty URL
findings.suggested_fix: In Security: 'Both `auth_introspection_endpoint` and `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` MUST be present and non-empty at startup or the verifier refuses to start.' In AC#5: append 'and that the verifier refuses to start when `auth_introspection_endpoint` is absent or empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2 (or add a dedicated assertion in AC3) to explicitly verify the `client_id` fallback chain (`client_id` present → used; absent → `sub`; both absent → `"unknown"`) and the `sub→subject` mapping (`sub` present → set; absent → `None`), naming these as observable oracles.
evidence: What: 'client_id→client_id (absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown")'
What: 'sub→subject (absent → None)'
AC2: 'an AccessToken with expected scopes, resource==auth_resource_server_url, and a positive expires_at' — client_id and subject not mentioned
findings.finding: The 'What' section specifies two field-mapping behaviors — (a) client_id→client_id with fallback to sub then 'unknown' when both are absent, and (b) sub→subject with fallback to None — but no acceptance criterion explicitly verifies either mapping. AC2 enumerates the checked fields as 'expected scopes, resource==auth_resource_server_url, and a positive expires_at', omitting client_id and subject from its observable oracle, leaving these described deliverables without a covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (field mapping paragraph) vs AC2
scenarios: An implementer omits the client_id fallback logic entirely and all ACs still pass, leaving a silent correctness gap in the produced AccessToken.
The subject field is never set on the AccessToken and no test catches it.
findings.suggested_fix:
In AC2, after 'and a positive expires_at', add: 'and that client_id is populated from the response client_id field (or falls back to sub, then "unknown" when both are absent) and subject is set from sub (None when absent).'
Add one assertion row in AC3 covering an active:true response where client_id is absent but sub is present (expect client_id==sub) and one where both are absent (expect client_id=="unknown").

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and implementation note) requiring that each introspection failure path (non-200, timeout, malformed response) emits a structured log event at WARNING/ERROR level including the error class and endpoint (but never the token or secret), to give operators an observable signal for AS degradation.
evidence: 'Tokens/secrets never logged' is the only logging mention — absence of any metric/log/trace for non-200, timeout, or malformed-response outcomes
No AC line requires an observable failure signal (e.g., a structured WARNING log with error class and endpoint, or a counter)
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (metrics, structured log events, or traces) for introspection-call failures. Operators cannot distinguish 'AS is down / timing out' from 'token legitimately rejected', making on-call triage and alerting on AS degradation impossible.
findings.location: Security section / Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers failure instrumentation
scenarios: The introspection AS returns 503 for 10 minutes; all MCP requests are denied; no alert fires and ops has no signal to distinguish this from a token-rejection storm.
A misconfigured endpoint silently times out every call; the only symptom is 100% auth failure with no log entry pointing to the AS.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section and a new AC line, specify: 'On any introspection failure (non-200, timeout, JSON parse error, missing active field), log a WARNING with fields: endpoint (URL, no query params), error_class (e.g. httpx.TimeoutException / status_code / JSONDecodeError), and request_id if available — never the token or secret.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and implementation note) specifying that IntrospectionTokenVerifier implements an async context-manager or explicit aclose() method that calls httpx_client.aclose(), and that the composite/factory that constructs it is responsible for calling it on shutdown.
evidence: 'Adapt the SDK simple-auth example' — the SDK example does not define a teardown path either
No AC requires an async context-manager protocol, __aclose__, or explicit client.aclose() call
No mention of how the httpx client is closed on server shutdown or verifier replacement
findings.finding: The plan creates an httpx client inside the IntrospectionTokenVerifier at construction time but defines no RETIRE/cleanup path — the client (and its underlying connection pool and OS sockets) is never explicitly closed, constituting a resource leak when the verifier is torn down (e.g., during test teardown, server restart, or config reload).
findings.location: Approach & Rationale — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'; no lifecycle AC or cleanup mention anywhere
scenarios: Integration tests create and discard multiple verifier instances; each leaks an httpx connection pool, causing ResourceWarning storms or socket exhaustion in CI.
A config reload creates a new verifier; the old httpx client's connection pool is never closed, leaking file descriptors until the process exits.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section add: 'IntrospectionTokenVerifier implements __aenter__/__aexit__ (or aclose()) delegating to the underlying httpx.AsyncClient, and the server startup/shutdown hooks call aclose() on teardown.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `httpx` explicitly to the `[mcp]` optional-dependency group in `pyproject.toml` (or verify with `pip show mcp | grep Requires` that `mcp>=1.9` transitively provides httpx, and document that finding in the plan).
evidence: Plan text: 'Reuse the already-present httpx.'
pyproject.toml line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — httpx not listed
pyproject.toml lines 62-66: core dependencies are pyyaml, jsonschema, referencing only
httpx present in venv (Partially-verified: as a transitive of the agents extra, not the mcp extra)
Evidence class: Partially-verified — httpx exists in the installed env but its presence via `nava-rebar[mcp]` alone is unconfirmed
findings.finding: The plan says 'Reuse the already-present httpx' but httpx is NOT declared in pyproject.toml's `[project.optional-dependencies.mcp]` (which only lists `mcp>=1.9`). httpx is available in the dev venv only as a transitive of the `[agents]` extra (pydantic-ai-slim). A plain `pip install nava-rebar[mcp]` would not guarantee httpx is present at runtime, causing an ImportError when the introspection verifier first fires.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'; pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies]
scenarios: Operator installs `nava-rebar[mcp]` (no agents extra) and enables introspection strategy — IntrospectionTokenVerifier import or first httpx.AsyncClient() call raises ImportError/ModuleNotFoundError at runtime.
CI matrix runs the MCP-only install path without agents extra — introspection unit tests fail to import.
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, change `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` to `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.24"]` (or whatever floor the verifier requires), ensuring `pip install nava-rebar[mcp]` always delivers httpx.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable oracle in AC#2 (or a dedicated AC) that verifies the `client_id` fallback chain (absent `client_id` + present `sub` → `sub`; absent both → `"unknown"`) and that `sub→subject` absent yields `None`, not an empty string or omitted field.
evidence: Plan 'What': 'client_id→client_id (absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown")' and 'sub→subject (absent → None)'
AC#2 tests: 'expected scopes, resource==auth_resource_server_url, and a positive expires_at' — client_id and subject fields are absent from the oracle list
No other AC references client_id or sub→subject mapping
findings.finding: The 'What' section defines the `client_id` fallback chain ('absent → falls back to `sub`, else `"unknown"`') and the `sub→subject` absent→`None` mapping as decision rules, but no AC tests or observable oracle covers these mappings. AC#2 explicitly names scopes, resource, and expires_at but omits client_id and subject. An executor can ship the fallback wrong and pass every AC.
findings.location: What section (field-mapping paragraph) and AC#2
scenarios: Executor hard-codes client_id to 'unknown' instead of falling back through sub — passes all ACs but violates the spec's stated fallback chain
Executor sets subject to empty string instead of None when sub is absent — passes all ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC#2: 'and that when `client_id` is absent from the response but `sub` is present, `AccessToken.client_id == sub`; and when both are absent, `client_id == "unknown"`; and that absent `sub` yields `subject is None`.' Add a corresponding stateful-fake scenario in the unit test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the Security section whether the 5s/10s timeout values are hardcoded constants in v1 (not operator-configurable) or whether named config keys will be added; if hardcoded, add a parenthetical like '(hardcoded in v1, not operator-configurable)'.
evidence: "a **bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)**"

The listed flat config keys (`auth_introspection_endpoint`, `auth_introspection_client_id`, `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`, `auth_introspection_allow_private_host`, `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`) do not include any timeout key, yet 'default' implies there is a non-default alternative.
findings.finding: The plan states 'bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)' but does not specify whether these values are hardcoded constants or operator-configurable via named config keys. An executor who reads 'default' as implying configurability may add undocumented config keys (e.g. `auth_introspection_connect_timeout_s`) that proliferate into MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json unintentionally; one who reads it as a fixed constant may omit configurability that operators need. The plan should resolve this ambiguity explicitly.
findings.location: Security section — 'a bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)'
scenarios: Executor adds timeout config keys that are not in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json, causing manifest drift test to fail.
Executor hardcodes the values and operators cannot tune timeouts for slow internal AS endpoints.
findings.suggested_fix: Add '(hardcoded in v1, not operator-configurable)' after 'default connect 5s / read 10s' to close the ambiguity without adding new config surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `auth_introspection_timeout_connect` and `auth_introspection_timeout_read` config keys (with defaults 5 and 10) to `McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`, and `server.json` so timeouts are operator-configurable without code changes.
evidence: 'a bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)' — asserted as a fixed value, not a configurable default
The plan registers config keys for endpoint, client_id, client_secret_env, allow_private_host, allow_missing_aud — but no keys for connect_timeout or read_timeout
Timeouts are operational parameters commonly needing tuning in high-latency or slow internal-AS deployments
findings.finding: The HTTP timeout values (connect 5s / read 10s) are hardcoded constants with no corresponding config keys, making them impossible to tune without a code change for deployments with slower or stricter internal Authorization Servers.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)'; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers timeout configurability)
scenarios: A deployment whose internal Keycloak is behind a VPN with >10s read latency fails every token introspection
A security-hardened deployment wants a 2s timeout to fail faster — no knob exists without a code change
findings.suggested_fix: Register two additional flat config keys: `auth_introspection_timeout_connect` (int, default 5) and `auth_introspection_timeout_read` (int, default 10). Add them alongside the other `auth_introspection_*` keys in McpConfig, _SECTIONS['mcp'], MCP_ENV_VARS, and server.json. Pass them to the httpx client at construction time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in Security or a new Reliability section) that the httpx client is constructed with an explicit connection-pool limit and that the plan either accepts the no-circuit-breaker risk explicitly for v1 (with a documented follow-on) or adds a simple consecutive-failure counter that fast-fails without waiting for the full timeout after N repeated AS errors.
evidence: 'every request re-introspects the token with the AS' — no circuit-breaker or fast-fail after repeated AS failures
Timeout of 10 s read × concurrent requests can accumulate; no connection-pool or concurrency limit specified for the httpx client
No mention of fallback or degraded mode if the AS is persistently down
findings.finding: When the introspection AS is unavailable or consistently slow, every in-flight MCP auth attempt blocks for up to 10 s (read timeout) before failing, with no circuit-breaker or degraded mode. Under even moderate concurrency this exhausts thread/async capacity and can deny service to unrelated operations sharing the event loop.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded HTTP timeout (default connect 5s / read 10s)'; Scope — 'No caching in v1'
scenarios: AS goes down during peak traffic; 50 concurrent requests each wait 10 s before failing, saturating the async event loop or thread pool and causing cascading latency for unrelated MCP tools.
A DNS misconfiguration causes every connect to hang for 5 s; auth latency becomes 5 s per request indefinitely with no circuit-breaker to fast-fail after N consecutive failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'The httpx client is created with limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=10, max_keepalive_connections=5). A circuit-breaker (N consecutive failures → immediate raise without waiting for timeout) is a documented v2 follow-on; v1 accepts the bounded blast radius at small scale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `CompositeTokenVerifier` exists in the installed `mcp` SDK (or is produced by S2) and update the component-test AC to name the exact importable class/module path, or replace with the actual composite mechanism S2 introduces.
evidence: resolve_symbol('CompositeTokenVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth.provider') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('CompositeTokenVerifier', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('CompositeTokenVerifier', 'mcp.server') → UNRESOLVED
grep for 'CompositeTokenVerifier' in src/ returns no matches
Plan text: 'the IntrospectionTokenVerifier inside a CompositeTokenVerifier, invoked through the composite's verify path'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'CompositeTokenVerifier' is a usable SDK construct (component AC: 'the IntrospectionTokenVerifier inside a CompositeTokenVerifier, invoked through the composite's verify path') but CompositeTokenVerifier is unresolvable in the installed mcp environment and absent from the repo codebase; if it doesn't exist as an importable symbol the component test tier as specified cannot be written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Component test bullet: 'the IntrospectionTokenVerifier inside a CompositeTokenVerifier'
scenarios: If CompositeTokenVerifier does not exist in the installed mcp SDK, the component test as written cannot be built — the implementer must either write their own composite wrapper or discover the SDK uses a different name/interface, which changes the scope of S4.
If CompositeTokenVerifier IS a forthcoming S2 artifact (written by this epic), then the plan needs to say so explicitly rather than implying it's an SDK import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` is created by S2 (making it a pre-existing file when S4 runs) or must be created by S4, and update the What section accordingly.
evidence: grep for '_mcp_auth' in src/rebar returns no matches
list_directory src/rebar shows no _mcp_auth.py file
Plan text implies adding to an existing file: 'Add an IntrospectionTokenVerifier to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py'
No explicit 'create new file' callout in the plan
findings.finding: The plan states `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` as the file to add `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` to ('Add an IntrospectionTokenVerifier to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py'), but this file does not exist in the current repository. The plan never explicitly states this file is created by S2 (its dependency) — it only says the 'strategy-selection config key introduced by S2'. If S2 does NOT create `_mcp_auth.py`, S4 must create it, but the plan treats it as a pre-existing target with no new-file callout.
findings.location: What section: 'Add an IntrospectionTokenVerifier to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py'
scenarios: If S2 creates _mcp_auth.py, the S4 implementer needs to know this is an S2 output — which the plan omits.
If S2 does NOT create _mcp_auth.py, S4 must create it (new file), but without being told so the AC omits the file-creation step from the observable oracle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'S2 creates `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`; S4 adds `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` to it.' or 'S4 creates `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` (new file) containing `IntrospectionTokenVerifier`.' depending on which story owns the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope or What section whether S4 edits `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` to wire the `introspection` strategy, or confirm that S2 fully owns the strategy-dispatch hookup and S4 only adds the verifier class and config fields.
evidence: Plan: 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key **introduced by S2**, this story's dependency — not a pre-existing seam)'
Plan: 'Scope: The introspection verifier only, on the S2 seam.'
`build_server()` in src/rebar/mcp_server.py (lines 241-270) contains no auth wiring today — it only calls register_read_tools/register_llm_tools/register_write_tools.
ABSENCE: The plan never states 'S4 does not edit build_server()' nor 'S2 provides the full strategy-dispatch wiring in _mcp_auth.py that build_server() then calls.'
findings.finding: The plan says the verifier is 'activated by including `introspection` in the `mcp.auth_strategies` list (the strategy-selection config key introduced by S2)' and is placed 'on the S2 seam,' but never states explicitly whether S4 edits `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` to register/dispatch the `introspection` strategy to `IntrospectionTokenVerifier`, or whether S2 fully owns that wiring. `build_server()` is currently auth-unaware (confirmed by reading the file), so some story must add the dispatch. If S4 owns it, `build_server()` is an unenumerated edit target; if S2 owns it, the plan should say so explicitly.
findings.location: What section / Scope section / AC5
scenarios: An executor implementing S4 without S2's code in hand cannot determine whether to edit build_server() or leave it to S2, risking either a duplicate edit conflict with S2 or a missing wiring step.

If S2's _mcp_auth.py exposes a factory function (e.g., build_composite_verifier()) that build_server() must call, and S4 doesn't edit build_server(), the introspection verifier is never reachable at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Scope: 'S2 owns the `build_server()` integration that dispatches `mcp.auth_strategies` entries to their verifier constructors; S4 registers only the new verifier class and its config fields, and S2's seam picks it up automatically.' Or, if S4 must touch `build_server()`, add it to the edit-target list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the SSRF guard to also validate the resolved IP address at request time (e.g., via a custom httpx transport/event hook that inspects the connected socket address against RFC 1918/loopback/link-local ranges), not only the configured hostname string at construction.
evidence: 'rejected at construction' — only a hostname-string check is implied
The plan does not mention a per-request IP check after DNS resolution
httpx.AsyncClient can be wrapped with a custom transport or event hook that inspects the resolved IP before connecting
findings.finding: The private/link-local host SSRF guard is specified only at construction time ('an `http://` endpoint is rejected at construction'; 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts … are rejected by default'). Unlike the JWKS story, this story's implementation directly controls its own httpx call and can perform a per-request resolved-IP check. A DNS rebinding attack (publicly-routable hostname at construction resolves to a private IP at request time) bypasses a hostname-string-only construction check. The plan provides no per-request IP validation, leaving the guard ineffective against this class of attack.
findings.location: Security section — 'Private/link-local endpoint hosts (RFC 1918, loopback, 169.254.0.0/16) are rejected by default; an operator … sets auth_introspection_allow_private_host=true to opt out.'
scenarios: Attacker controls DNS for the configured introspection endpoint hostname; at construction time DNS returns a public IP (check passes), at request time DNS returns 169.254.169.254 or 10.0.0.1 (SSRF succeeds against internal services).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the httpx client lifecycle in the Approach section: create a single `httpx.AsyncClient` at verifier construction (for connection-pool reuse) and call `await client.aclose()` on verifier teardown, matching the existing runner.py pattern.
evidence: Plan says 'reuse the already-present httpx' with no mention of client instantiation strategy
Existing codebase (runner.py) explicitly manages httpx.AsyncClient teardown: 'Close the per-run retrying httpx.AsyncClient to close on teardown (story arcticduck); aclose() is…'
No AC or approach text covers client lifecycle or teardown
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the lifecycle of the httpx client instance: whether a new client is created per-request or a single shared client is constructed once and reused. Per-request creation loses TCP connection pooling (re-handshakes TLS on every token verification, adding ~100–300 ms latency under load). A shared client requires a teardown path (aclose()) when the verifier is shut down, which is not mentioned. This is a mechanism gap with real performance and resource-leak consequences.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section / What section
scenarios: Per-request client creation: TLS re-handshake on every introspection call (each MCP request), multiplying latency under load.
Shared client, no teardown: file-descriptor/connection-pool leak over time; resource exhaustion in long-running servers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'A single `httpx.AsyncClient` is constructed at verifier init and reused across requests (TLS connection pool reuse). Its `aclose()` is called when the verifier is shut down, following the runner.py teardown pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC#5 whether `CompositeTokenVerifier` is a repo-local class introduced by S2 (name the import path, e.g. `rebar._mcp_auth.CompositeTokenVerifier`) or an SDK export — resolve the unverified capability assertion before the component test is written.
evidence: AC#5 text: 'the `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` inside a `CompositeTokenVerifier`, invoked through the composite's verify path'
UNRESOLVED: CompositeTokenVerifier in mcp.server.auth.provider
UNRESOLVED: CompositeTokenVerifier in mcp (top-level)
Plan body (What section) says S2 introduces the strategy-selection seam but does not state S2 exports or names a CompositeTokenVerifier class
Plan Scope section: 'Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S5/S6' — S2 as a dependency is named but its exported API surface is not enumerated here
findings.finding: `CompositeTokenVerifier` is referenced in the Component Test AC as the harness in which `IntrospectionTokenVerifier` is exercised, but the plan never states whether this class is introduced by S2 (repo-local) or is an exported SDK symbol — and it is UNRESOLVED in every probed mcp namespace (`mcp`, `mcp.server.auth.provider`, `mcp.server.auth.middleware`, `mcp.server.auth.handlers`). If it is SDK-only and non-existent, the component test harness design is broken before an executor starts.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#5 (Component test)
scenarios: Executor implements AC#5 assuming CompositeTokenVerifier is an mcp SDK export, discovers it doesn't exist, and has to invent the composite wrapper from scratch or defer to S2.
CompositeTokenVerifier is defined in S2 (repo-local _mcp_auth.py) but this plan never names it as a dependency artifact — the AC is ambiguous about where to import it from.
findings.suggested_fix: If S2 introduces CompositeTokenVerifier in `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, add a parenthetical to AC#5: '(using the repo-local `rebar._mcp_auth.CompositeTokenVerifier` introduced by S2)'. If the intent is to use the SDK class, verify it is exported and name the exact import path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement to the Security section that TLS certificate verification is hardcoded to enabled (`verify=True` in httpx) and is NOT operator-configurable — no `auth_introspection_tls_verify` escape hatch — so the HTTPS transport guarantee cannot be silently removed.
evidence: 'an http:// endpoint is rejected at construction' — scheme check is specified
No mention of httpx `verify=True` being required or `verify=False` being prohibited
The plan does not specify that TLS certificate validation is a non-negotiable, non-configurable property of the introspection client
findings.finding: The plan mandates HTTPS-only via construction-time rejection of `http://` endpoints but does not explicitly affirm that TLS certificate verification is enforced and non-configurable. An httpx client can be instantiated with `verify=False`, silently defeating the HTTPS security contract even while the scheme check passes.
findings.location: Security section — 'Call the introspection endpoint over authenticated TLS … HTTPS-only (an http:// endpoint is rejected at construction)'
scenarios: A developer or operator, intending to test against a local AS with a self-signed cert, sets httpx `verify=False` in the implementation; this silently removes the HTTPS security guarantee for all deployments.
A future contributor adds a `auth_introspection_tls_verify` config key for 'convenience', not realising the plan intended to make cert validation non-negotiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'TLS certificate verification is always enabled (`verify=True`); there is no operator config key to disable it. A self-signed or internal CA cert must be made trusted at the OS/container level, not by disabling verification.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
